Where there is truth of grace, it will increase. The path of the righteous shines brighter and brighter toward a perfect day. Where grace increases, God must have all the glory. As faith grows, love will abound, because faith works through love. It demonstrates faith and patience, which can be presented as an example for others when trials from God and persecution from people quicken the exercise of those graces; for the patience and faith about which the apostle rejoiced sustained them and enabled them to endure all their tribulations.

If religion is worth anything, it is worth everything; and those who have no religion, or none that is worthwhile, do not know how to value it, nor can they find themselves willing to suffer for it. We cannot, by our sufferings or services, merit heaven; but through our patience under suffering, we are prepared for the promised joy. Nothing more strongly marks a person for eternal ruin than a spirit of persecution and hostility toward God’s name and people. God will trouble those who trouble his people. And there is rest for God’s people—rest from sin and sorrow. The certainty of future reward is proven by God's righteousness. This should terrify wicked people and support the righteous. Faith, looking to the great day, is enabled partly to understand the book of providence, which appears confused to unbelievers. The Lord Jesus will appear in that day from heaven. He will come in glory and power. His light will be piercing, and his power consuming, to all who are found as chaff. This appearance will be terrible to those who do not know God, especially those who rebel against revelation and disobey the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ. This is the great crime of many—the gospel is revealed, and they will not believe it; or if they pretend to believe, they will not obey it. Believing the truths of the gospel leads to obeying its commands. Though sinners may be spared for a long time, they will be punished at last. They did sin’s work and must receive sin's wages. Here God punishes sinners by creatures as instruments; but then, it will be destruction from the Almighty—and who knows the power of his anger? It will be a joyful day for some, for the saints, those who believe and obey the gospel. In that bright and blessed day, Christ Jesus will be glorified and admired by his saints. And Christ will be glorified and admired in them. His grace and power will be shown when it appears what he has purchased, wrought, and bestowed upon those who believe in him. Lord, if the glory put upon your saints will be so admired, how much more should you be admired as the Giver of that glory! The glory of your justice in the damnation of the wicked will be admired, but not as the glory of your mercy in the salvation of believers. How will this strike the adoring angels with holy admiration and transport your admiring saints with eternal rapture! The least believer will enjoy more than the most generous heart can imagine while we are here; Christ will be admired in all those who believe, the least believer not excepted.

Believing thoughts and expectations of the second coming of Christ should lead us to pray to God more for ourselves and others. If there is any good in us, it is due to his goodness, and therefore it is called grace. There are many purposes of grace and goodwill in God toward his people, and the apostle prays that God would complete in them the work of faith with power—this enables every other good work. The power of God not only begins but carries on the work of faith. This is the great purpose and design of the grace of our God and Lord Jesus Christ, which is made known to us and worked in us.
